prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Neuroscience / Neural Decoding.
Below are 8 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
Generative fMRI-to-video decoders now improve perceptual fidelity with visual-cortex-inspired priors, but low-resolution neural measurements and strong diffusion priors can hallucinate content not actually supported by brain activity. What uncertainty-calibrated decoding architecture would disentangle neural evidence from generative prior, generalize across subjects and time, and produce mechanistically interpretable reconstructions of dynamic visual perception?

[Paper 1] Deep image reconstruction from human brain activity
Brief Abstract: This paper introduced a major shift from hand-crafted visual features to deep neural network feature decoding for image reconstruction from fMRI. By decoding hierarchical DNN features from brain activity and optimizing images to match them, it produced recognizable reconstructions of natural images, artificial shapes, and even mental imagery.

[Paper 2] High-resolution image reconstruction with latent diffusion models from human brain activity
Brief Abstract: This paper showed that latent diffusion models can reconstruct high-resolution images from fMRI with minimal additional training by mapping brain activity to latent and text-conditioning spaces. It marked a transition from optimization over decoded features to leveraging powerful pretrained generative priors for semantically rich reconstruction.

[Paper 3] Brain-Diffuser: Natural scene reconstruction from fMRI signals using generative latent diffusion
Brief Abstract: Brain-Diffuser adapted latent diffusion for natural-scene reconstruction from fMRI and helped establish diffusion-based decoding as a broader methodological family beyond a single implementation. It demonstrated that diffusion priors could be integrated into end-to-end neural decoding pipelines for visually plausible reconstructions.

[Paper 4] Neurons: Emulating the Human Visual Cortex Improves Fidelity and Interpretability in fMRI-to-Video Reconstruction
Brief Abstract: Paper B introduces a biologically inspired fMRI-to-video reconstruction framework that emulates the hierarchical human visual cortex to improve both reconstruction fidelity and interpretability for dynamic visual stimuli.

[Paper 5] Brain-Score: Which Artificial Neural Network for Object Recognition is most Brain-Like?
Brief Abstract: This paper systematized the comparison between artificial neural networks and primate visual representations by introducing Brain-Score, a benchmark based on neural and behavioral predictivity. It helped formalize the idea that better ANN-brain alignment can guide both neuroscience models and decoding feature spaces.

[Paper 6] MindEye: fMRI-to-Image with Contrastive Retrieval and Iterative Reconstruction
Brief Abstract: MindEye combined contrastive retrieval with diffusion-based reconstruction to improve both semantic matching and image synthesis from fMRI. It emphasized learned multimodal embedding alignment and iterative reconstruction, helping bridge retrieval-style decoding and generative decoding.

[Paper 7] Identifying natural images from human brain activity
Brief Abstract: This paper showed that fMRI activity in human visual cortex can be used to identify which natural image a subject saw from a large candidate set. It established a practical voxel-wise encoding/decoding framework linking visual features to cortical responses and back to stimulus inference.

[Paper 8] Reconstructing visual experiences from brain activity evoked by natural movies
Brief Abstract: This work extended visual decoding from image identification to approximate reconstruction of seen natural movies from fMRI signals. It used motion-energy encoding models and a large natural movie prior to synthesize dynamic visual content from cortical activity.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}
